data.text: I'll check the existing ADR directory to find the right numbering, then read the referenced specs before writing the document.
The last ADR is 025, so this will be ADR-026. Let me read the referenced specs before writing.

data.output:
1	---
2	ddx:
3	  id: TD-031
4	  depends_on:
5	    - FEAT-004
6	    - SD-004
7	    - ADR-004
8	---
9	# Technical Design: Bead State Machine and Naming-Role Decisions
10	
11	## Purpose
12	
13	This TD is the canonical reference for what bead state means in DDx and where
14	each observed name in the codebase belongs. It exists because schema, docs,
15	code, and persisted data have drifted: the bead-record JSON Schema enumerates
16	six statuses (the bd/br canonical set), FEAT-004 still documents three, and
17	several legacy/backcompat names appear in code paths (`done`, `needs_human`,
18	`pending`, `ready`, `review`, `needs_investigation`) without a single decision
19	record explaining whether each is a status, a derived queue category, an
20	event kind, a label, or worker state.
21	
22	The five in-flight hygiene beads each introduce new state-machine vocabulary:
23	
24	- ddx-b24e9630 — `no_changes_*` outcome verification (NoChangesContract)
25	- ddx-3c154349 — auto-triage of stuck beads (TriageContract)
26	- ddx-aede917d — drain pause on quota exhaustion (QuotaPauseContract)
27	- ddx-c6e3db02 — rate-limit retry behavior (RateLimitRetryContract)
28	- ddx-da11a34a — main-git/tracker lock contention handling
29	  (LockContentionContract)
30	
31	Without one TD that nails down the categories and the transition matrix,
32	those five beads will each independently invent overlapping vocabulary.
33	
34	This TD authors the design only. The mechanical reconciliation
35	(schema-vs-FEAT-004 alignment, code rename of non-status names, CI guards,
36	hygiene-bead AC substitution, migration survey) is filed as sibling beads.
37	
38	## Critical Constraint (per ADR-004)
39	
40	The bead-record envelope must remain compatible with the bd/br interchange
41	contract. The persisted `status` enum is **fixed** at the bd/br canonical six
42	values:
43	
44	```
45	open, in_progress, closed, blocked, proposed, cancelled
46	```
47	
48	DDx-specific execution semantics live in **labels**, **events**, or the
49	preserved-extras **Extra** map — never in new statuses. Adding a new persisted
50	status requires upstream bd/br coordination plus an ADR-004 amendment. This
51	TD does not authorize any such addition.
52	
53	## Today's Observed State (verified)
54	
55	- `cli/internal/bead/schema/bead-record.schema.json` enumerates six values.
56	- `docs/helix/01-frame/features/FEAT-004-beads.md` line 65 still documents
57	  three values (`open, in_progress, closed`). FEAT-004 lags the schema.
58	- `ddx bead list --json | jq -r '.[].status' | sort -u` on real projects
59	  returns only `open`, `in_progress`, `closed` for active rows today —
60	  `blocked`, `proposed`, and `cancelled` are schema-allowed but unused in
61	  the current active queue.
62	- The legacy/backcompat names `done`, `needs_human`, `pending`, `ready`, `review`,
63	  `needs_investigation` appear in code paths but are **not** persisted bead
64	  statuses today. Each falls into one of the other categories defined below.
65	
66	## Sections
67	
68	The remainder of this TD is structured as the eleven design sections required
69	by the originating bead, followed by five per-hygiene-bead contract
70	subsections.
71	
72	---
73	
74	## 1. Category Taxonomy
75	
76	Every state-machine name observed in DDx falls into exactly one of the
77	following categories. The category determines where the name is allowed to
78	live (schema, label namespace, event kind, queue derivation, or worker
79	process state).
80

1	---
2	ddx:
3	  id: SD-025
4	  depends_on:
5	    - FEAT-005
6	    - FEAT-006
7	    - FEAT-010
8	---
9	# Solution Design: Task Execution Lifecycle
10	
11	## Purpose
12	
13	This design turns FEAT-010's task execution lifecycle into an implementation
14	shape. It is intentionally below the feature spec and above code-level storage
15	types: enough detail to keep the `run`, `try`, and `work` layers aligned
16	without re-opening the routing boundary in FEAT-006.
17	
18	## Layer Model
19	
20	Task execution is composed from named layers:
21	
22	| Layer | Public verb | Meaning |
23	| --- | --- | --- |
24	| 1 | `ddx run` | one upstream agent invocation |
25	| 2 | `ddx try <bead>` | one bead attempt in an isolated worktree |
26	| 3 | `ddx work` | one mechanical queue-drain loop |
27	
28	Each higher layer references child records from the layer beneath it. Comparison,
29	replay, benchmark, review, and artifact-regeneration workflows are compositions
30	over the task execution lifecycle, not new run kinds.
31	
32	## Record Shape
33	
34	Every record lives under `.ddx/runs/<run-id>/record.json`. Large bodies are
35	attachments in the same directory.
36	
37	```json
38	{
39	  "run_id": "run_20260430T120102Z_01HV...",
40	  "layer": 2,
41	  "parent_run_id": "run_20260430T115900Z_01HV...",
42	  "started_at": "2026-04-30T12:01:02Z",
43	  "finished_at": "2026-04-30T12:04:12Z",
44	  "terminal_status": "success",
45	  "actor": "erik",
46	  "host": "sindri",
47	  "git_revision": "abc123",
48	  "ddx_version": "0.0.0-dev",
49	  "produces_artifact": null,
50	  "attachments": [
51	    {"name": "prompt", "path": "prompt.md", "media_type": "text/markdown"},
52	    {"name": "result", "path": "result.json", "media_type": "application/json"}
53	  ],
54	  "layer1": null,
55	  "layer2": {
56	    "bead_id": "ddx-12345678",
57	    "base_revision": "abc123",
58	    "result_revision": "def456",
59	    "worktree_path": ".ddx/worktrees/ddx-12345678-run_...",
60	    "finalization": "merge",
61	    "child_run_ids": ["run_20260430T120104Z_01HV..."],
62	    "gate_summary": "go test ./internal/agent"
63	  },
64	  "layer3": null
65	}
66	```
67	
68	Rules:
69	
70	- `layer` is the discriminator; exactly one of `layer1`, `layer2`, or `layer3`
71	  is populated.
72	- `parent_run_id` points upward when a run is nested; child ids live in the
73	  layer extension for efficient drill-down.
74	- `terminal_status` is the substrate outcome. Domain-specific detail remains in
75	  the layer extension and attachments.
76	- `produces_artifact` is set when a run regenerates an artifact; FEAT-007 reads
77	  it as provenance for `generated_by`.
78	- Records are append-only. Correction means writing a new record or a separate
79	  review/annotation artifact, not editing a historical record.
80

data.text: Let me read the most recent ADR to match the format.

data.output:
1	---
2	ddx:
3	  id: ADR-025
4	  depends_on:
5	    - FEAT-027
6	    - TD-036
7	    - SPIKE-2026-05-06-prose-checker-engine-selection
8	    - SPIKE-2026-05-06-vale-internal-engine
9	    - SPIKE-2026-05-06-prose-workflow-integration
10	---
11	# ADR-025: Prose Checker Engine
12	
13	**Status:** Accepted
14	**Date:** 2026-05-06
15	**Authors:** operator decision after prose checker spikes
16	
17	## Context
18	
19	DDx needs one opinionated prose-quality path. The public surface is
20	`ddx doc prose`; users should not choose between a matrix of prose tools or
21	maintain project-local checker setup.
22	
23	The prose checker engine selection and Vale integration spikes narrowed the
24	viable choices to:
25	
26	1. Extend the embedded Go checker.
27	2. Use Vale as the deterministic engine behind DDx's prose surface.
28	
29	Node-, Java-, and Python-distributed prose tools were rejected for the default
30	path because they violate DDx's installation model. Vale is written in Go and
31	ships official platform release binaries, but its reusable implementation
32	packages are internal to its module. DDx should therefore treat Vale as a
33	pinned CLI dependency, not as an imported Go library.
34	
35	## Decision
36	
37	DDx will use Vale as the deterministic prose checker engine behind
38	`ddx doc prose`, with DDx owning the user-facing command, defaults, rule
39	packaging, finding schema, workflow hooks, and diagnostics.
40	
41	Vale is installed through Vale's normal release/install channel, not built by
42	DDx at runtime and not wrapped in a DDx-managed package-manager flow. DDx pins
43	the supported Vale version and verifies the binary through `ddx doctor`.
44	
45	The initial pinned version is:
46	
47	```text
48	vale 3.13.0
49	```
50	
51	The supported release artifacts and checksums are:
52	
53	```text
54	4378ee4bc7c2493760826270e55d5569cda35d7c89582e9fdc3070e2a1089193  vale_3.13.0_Linux_64-bit.tar.gz
55	2134f23e7afbdf70b44272e6d3b5f26e85972340faa1e2a2b194358cf2892d84  vale_3.13.0_Linux_arm64.tar.gz
56	9f2991092579e85dd5be082c691b7b14ddbcd7c65477a6ff44b5f5e8dc3a9079  vale_3.13.0_macOS_64-bit.tar.gz
57	2e89bd82cadfffa6abebda80a141529db2799df5d4197e6aa0489a4d711d8a3b  vale_3.13.0_macOS_arm64.tar.gz
58	fb1141183d783ef1b9278ea5b1cc04e85801256b1539fc47147b90f6bf082341  vale_3.13.0_Windows_64-bit.zip
59	```
60	
61	## DDx Responsibilities
62	
63	DDx owns:
64	
65	- the `ddx doc prose` command and output format;
66	- the default document scope (`docs/**`);
67	- DDx-specific Vale rules for execution-useful prose and AI-slop avoidance;
68	- temporary/generated Vale config for the DDx rule pack;
69	- normalization from Vale JSON to DDx findings;
70	- workflow integration and advisory policy;
71	- `ddx doctor` checks for a supported Vale binary on `PATH`.
72	
73	DDx does not expose Vale as a public runner choice. Vale is an implementation
74	detail behind DDx's prose command.
75	
76	## Doctor Contract
77	
78	`ddx doctor` must check:
79	
80	1. `vale` is available on `PATH`;
81	2. `vale --version` reports the pinned supported version;
82	3. the installed binary path is displayed when verbose output is requested;
83	4. an unsupported or missing Vale binary is reported as a prose-checker
84	   diagnostic with remediation that points to Vale's official install/release
85	   channel and the pinned version.
86	
87	This check is non-critical until the Vale-backed `ddx doc prose` implementation
88	lands. Once Vale is the active prose engine, a missing or unsupported Vale
89	binary is a prose-checker setup failure, not an invitation for DDx to silently
90	switch engines.
91	
92	## Consequences
93	
94	- DDx keeps its opinionated prose surface while relying on a mature
95	  markup-aware checker engine.
96	- Operators install Vale using the upstream-supported path for their platform.
97	- DDx avoids maintaining embedded third-party binaries inside the DDx release
98	  artifact.
99	- DDx must keep the pinned Vale version current through ordinary dependency
100	  maintenance.
101	- The embedded checker becomes a development fallback and test reference, not
102	  the long-term default engine.
103	
104	## Rejected Options
105	
106	### Public runner selection
107	
108	Rejected. DDx should not expose `embedded | vale | textlint | language-tool`
109	style choices for normal prose checking.
110	
111	### Build Vale during DDx install
112	
113	Rejected. A spike showed source builds can fail due transitive parser/build
114	constraints. DDx's install path should not compile Vale.
115	
116	### Bundle platform Vale binaries inside DDx
117	
118	Rejected for now. It keeps the user experience tight but increases release
119	artifact size and platform-specific maintenance. The path can be revisited if
120	the upstream install channel proves unreliable.
121	
122	### Import Vale as a Go library
123	
124	Rejected for now. Vale's core linter packages are under Go `internal/`
125	directories, so DDx cannot import them as a stable public API.
126	
127	### Node, Java, or Python prose tools
128	
129	Rejected for the default path. They may inform rule design, but they do not fit
130	DDx's default installation model.
131	
132

data.text: Now I have enough context. Let me write the ADR.

input.content:
---
ddx:
  id: ADR-026
  depends_on:
    - ADR-004
    - TD-031
    - SD-025
---
# ADR-026: Evidence Storage Architecture (3-Tier)

**Status:** Accepted
**Date:** 2026-05-10
**Authors:** operator decision after execution-evidence cleanup discoveries

## 1. Context

DDx accumulates per-attempt evidence during bead execution — manifest.json,
prompt.md, result.json, reviewer-stream.log, agent stream logs, and worker
runtime state — without a documented retention or storage architecture.

The current implementation is inconsistent:

- `.ddx/executions/` dirs are tracked in git via explicit un-ignore rules,
  accumulating large volumes of per-attempt files (12,096 tracked execution
  files discovered in a single audit).
- `.ddx/agent-logs/` contains unbounded raw model-stream data (860 MB observed
  locally) but is gitignored and has no retention policy.
- `.ddx/workers/` is neither gitignored nor actively tracked; its status is
  undefined.
- No central metrics extraction exists; cross-attempt queries require fanning
  out reads across thousands of individual files.
- No ADR, spec, or documentation describes the intended storage model, what
  belongs in git, or how long local artifacts are retained.

This gap means every new execution feature independently invents conventions,
leading to repo bloat and unqueryable evidence. A formal architecture is needed
to balance *reconstruct-the-past* capability against *repo bloat*, and to
enable optimization analysis (model / prompt / harness / spec performance)
without forcing all detailed evidence into git.

This ADR cross-references TD-031 §5 (bead event vocabulary) and SD-025
(execution lifecycle record shape).

## 2. Decision

DDx adopts a three-tier evidence storage model:

| Tier | Location | What lives here | Durability |
|------|----------|-----------------|------------|
| Git | `.ddx/beads.jsonl`, `.ddx/attachments/`, `.ddx/metrics/` | Durable, small, structured state | Permanent; committed |
| Local disk | `.ddx/executions/`, `.ddx/agent-logs/`, `.ddx/workers/` | Detailed, ephemeral evidence bundles | 90-day default; never committed |
| External archive | operator-configured mirror hook | Full evidence bundles for long-term retention | Operator-managed |

Git holds only what is queryable, durable, and small. Local disk holds the
rich per-attempt detail needed for debugging, review, and short-term replay.
External archive is available when operators need long-term access to the
detailed evidence but is not a built-in DDx backend.

## 3. Principles

### P1 — Git holds durable, small, structured state

Git is the authoritative store for:

- **Bead rows** (`.ddx/beads.jsonl`): one row per bead, ~2–5 KB each, the
  complete work-item record.
- **Closed-bead events** (`.ddx/attachments/<bead-id>/events.jsonl`): lifecycle
  events for each closed bead (~1–3 KB), written by `ddx bead migrate`.
- **Aggregate per-attempt metrics** (`.ddx/metrics/attempts.jsonl`): one row
  per bead attempt, ~300 bytes/attempt, structured fields enabling cross-bead
  queries without file-fanning.
- **Code, docs, ADRs, TDs, FEATs**: all primary design and implementation
  artifacts.

Nothing else belongs in git. The un-ignore rules for `.ddx/executions/` are a
historical accident that this ADR obsoletes; they are removed by sister bead
B30.

Rationale: git history is immutable and replicated. Storing large binary or
repetitive data in git permanently increases clone size, degrades CI cache
performance, and cannot be undone without history-rewriting operations that
violate DDx's merge policy.

### P2 — Local disk holds detailed, ephemeral, retention-bounded evidence

The local disk stores the full evidence bundle for each execution attempt and
the raw model-stream output. These are per-machine and are never committed to
git.

Paths:

- `.ddx/executions/<attempt-id>/` — full evidence bundle:
  `prompt.md`, `manifest.json`, `result.json`, `usage.json`, `checks.json`,
  `reviewer-stream.log`, `decisions.md`, `no_changes_rationale.txt`, etc.
- `.ddx/agent-logs/agent-*.jsonl` — raw model stream, one file per agent run.
- `.ddx/workers/worker-*/` — per-worker runtime state during active execution.

Default retention: **90 days** from attempt completion. Configurable via
`.ddx/config.yaml` key `evidence.retention_days`.

Rationale: detailed evidence is primarily useful immediately after an attempt
(debugging, reviewer inspection) and during short-term replay. After 90 days
the evidence has low marginal value for most operators, and unbounded local
accumulation degrades disk usage without proportionate benefit. Operators who
need longer retention use the external archive tier (P3).

### P3 — External archive is operator-configured, not built-in

DDx provides a **mirror hook** — a configurable command invoked after each
attempt completes — that operators can use to copy the full evidence bundle to
an external backend. No built-in archive backend is mandated.

Hook interface (`.ddx/config.yaml`):

```yaml
evidence:
  retention_days: 90
  archive_hook: ""   # shell command; receives attempt-id as $1
```

When `archive_hook` is set, DDx invokes it synchronously after writing the
evidence bundle and before applying local retention. The hook is responsible
for any remote upload, indexing, or transformation. A non-zero hook exit does
not abort the attempt but is logged as a warning.

Default backends left to operators:

- **local-dir copy** (e.g., `cp -r .ddx/executions/$1 /mnt/evidence/`) — the
  simplest, no external dependency.
- **remote** (e.g., S3 via `aws s3 sync`) — deferred to operator configuration.

Rationale: DDx should not mandate a specific remote storage system. Archive
needs vary widely across operator environments (air-gapped, cloud-native,
regulated). A hook point is the minimal, composable interface that avoids
baking in external service dependencies while still enabling long-term
retention workflows.

### P4 — Aggregate metrics are denormalized, queryable, and durable

`.ddx/metrics/attempts.jsonl` is the analytics table for cross-bead
optimization analysis. Each row captures one bead attempt with structured
fields designed for filtering and aggregation without loading individual
evidence bundles.

Minimum required fields per row:

```json
{
  "attempt_id":    "20260510T214323-595fc029",
  "bead_id":       "ddx-7e9e5e1a",
  "started_at":    "2026-05-10T21:43:23Z",
  "finished_at":   "2026-05-10T22:01:45Z",
  "outcome":       "success",
  "harness":       "claude",
  "model":         "claude-sonnet-4-6",
  "host":          "sindri",
  "operator":      "erik",
  "labels":        ["phase:6", "area:docs"],
  "spec_id":       "ADR-026",
  "token_input":   12400,
  "token_output":  3200,
  "duration_s":    1102
}
```

This file is append-only. Rows are written by `ddx agent execute-bead` at
attempt completion, whether the attempt succeeds, is preserved, or produces
no changes. The `outcome` field maps to the terminal status vocabulary in
TD-031 §5.

Rationale: fanning out reads across thousands of `manifest.json` files is
impractical for batch analysis. A single append-only JSONL file enables
`ddx jq` queries, `grep`, and external analytics tools without per-file
traversal. Denormalization is intentional — correctness requires that each row
is self-contained at write time.

## 4. Per-Bead Git Footprint

| Artifact | Size | Notes |
|----------|------|-------|
| Bead row in `.ddx/beads.jsonl` | ~2–5 KB | Written at creation |
| Events file `.ddx/attachments/<id>/events.jsonl` | ~1–3 KB | Written by `ddx bead migrate` after close |
| Metrics rows in `.ddx/metrics/attempts.jsonl` | ~300 bytes × N attempts | Written at each attempt completion |

For a typical bead with 3 attempts:
- Git footprint: **~5–8 KB tracked**
- Current baseline (12,096 execution files + large agent-logs tracked): **~95 KB equivalent per bead**
- Reduction: approximately **20×**

The reduction comes entirely from moving the per-attempt evidence bundles
(prompt.md, result.json, stream logs, reviewer output) off git and onto local
disk, retaining only the structured metrics row in git.

## 5. Local Retention

**Default:** 90 days from `finished_at` in the attempt manifest.

**Override:**

```yaml
# .ddx/config.yaml
evidence:
  retention_days: 90   # set to 0 to disable automatic eviction
```

Setting `retention_days: 0` disables automatic eviction entirely. Operators
are responsible for managing disk usage in that mode.

Retention enforcement runs:

1. At the start of each `ddx work` / `ddx agent execute-loop` invocation, as a
   background pass before the first attempt begins.
2. On explicit `ddx agent gc` invocation (planned; sister bead B29).

Only directories under `.ddx/executions/` and files under `.ddx/agent-logs/`
older than the retention window are removed. Active worktrees and
`.ddx/workers/` dirs for currently-running executions are never touched.

## 6. External Archive Interface

The archive hook is a shell command stored in `.ddx/config.yaml` under
`evidence.archive_hook`. DDx passes the attempt ID as the first positional
argument (`$1`). The attempt evidence directory is always
`.ddx/executions/$1/`.

Example — copy to local archive directory:

```bash
# .ddx/config.yaml
evidence:
  archive_hook: "cp -r .ddx/executions/$1 /mnt/evidence/ddx/"
```

Example — upload to S3:

```bash
evidence:
  archive_hook: "aws s3 sync .ddx/executions/$1 s3://my-bucket/ddx-evidence/$1"
```

DDx does not provide built-in S3, GCS, or Azure backends. The hook interface
is the complete external-archive API surface for this version of the ADR.
Remote backend implementations (built-in `ddx evidence push` command) are
explicitly deferred and not part of this decision.

## 7. Cross-Machine Considerations

`.ddx/metrics/attempts.jsonl` operates under a **single-machine assumption**:
each operator machine writes rows for its own attempts and commits them to the
shared git repository. Merge conflicts on the metrics file are resolved by
appending (each row is independent; a line-level merge produces a superset of
both machines' rows and is always correct).

This model is sufficient for single-operator and small-team workflows where
each machine pushes to a shared repository. It breaks down when:

- Multiple machines execute attempts concurrently and attempt simultaneous
  pushes (requires coordination or last-write-wins semantics at the file level).
- Federation across repositories is needed (FEAT-007 topology).

**Forward pointer:** when federation lands (ADR-007), `.ddx/metrics/` will be
partitioned by `host` or `operator` into per-origin shards
(`.ddx/metrics/attempts-<host>.jsonl`), assembled by the federation layer for
cross-repo queries. The schema of individual rows does not change; only the
file-per-host sharding is added. Implementations building on this ADR should
treat the single-file assumption as a simplification, not a permanent
constraint.

## 8. Migration Plan

This ADR establishes the architecture. The following sister beads implement it:

| Bead | Scope |
|------|-------|
| **B29** — retention enforcement | Implement `evidence.retention_days` eviction in `ddx agent execute-loop` and `ddx agent gc`. |
| **B30** — gitignore flip | Remove the un-ignore rules for `.ddx/executions/`; add `.ddx/executions/`, `.ddx/agent-logs/`, and `.ddx/workers/` to `.gitignore`. |
| **B31** — mirror hook | Implement `evidence.archive_hook` invocation in `ddx agent execute-bead` post-attempt path. |
| **metrics-extraction** | Implement `.ddx/metrics/attempts.jsonl` append in `ddx agent execute-bead`; define the complete row schema. |
| **analyze CLI** | Implement `ddx analyze` (or `ddx metrics`) command for querying `attempts.jsonl`. |

Migration of the existing tracked execution files in git is covered by B30
and requires a one-time history-neutral commit that removes the un-ignored
execution files and adds the correct gitignore entries. No rewrite of git
history is performed.

## Consequences

- Per-bead git footprint drops ~20× for new attempts immediately after B30.
- Cross-attempt optimization analysis becomes possible once the
  metrics-extraction bead lands.
- Operators who need long-term evidence retention configure the mirror hook;
  no action is needed for the default 90-day local-disk path.
- The single-machine assumption is an explicit, documented constraint rather
  than an implicit limitation; it will be revisited when federation is
  implemented.
- Detailed evidence (prompt.md, agent stream logs) is no longer available via
  git clone on machines that have exceeded the retention window and have not
  configured an external archive.

data.text:
All 6 ACs satisfied:

1. `docs/helix/02-design/adr/ADR-026-evidence-storage-architecture.md` exists with all 8 sections.
2. Principles P1–P4 documented with rationale in §3.
3. Per-bead git footprint table in §4 shows ~5–8 KB tracked vs ~95 KB today (~20× reduction).
4. 90-day default and `evidence.retention_days` override documented in §5.
5. Single-machine assumption for `attempts.jsonl` with forward pointer to federation-time partitioning in §7.
6. `lefthook run pre-commit` passed clean.
